coredata.dc:title: Investigation of visible-light-driven photocatalytic tetracycline degradation via carbon dots modified porous ZnSnO3 cubes: Mechanism and degradation pathway
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Feng
Huang, Xiliu
Chen, Zhihao
Sun, Haoran
Shi, Weilong
coredata.dc:description: It is highly desired to enhance the photocatalytic activity of ZnSnO3 owing to its potential application prospects in the photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics in water, such as the low cost, non-toxicity, simple preparation, although ZnSnO3 possesses wide band gap (~3.2 eV). Herein, a novel carbon dots (CDs) modified porous ZnSnO3 cubic photocatalyst was designed and synthesized using a simple precipitation-calcination method, which was to utilize the up-conversion photoluminescence performance and the ability of electronic transfer of CDs, further broadening the visible light absorption range of the photocatalyst and accelerating the separation of the electron-hole pairs. The experimental results indicate that with the optimal CDs loading (3 wt%), the CDs modified ZnSnO3 exhibits 21 times higher degradation kinetic towards tetracycline (TC) than pure ZnSnO3. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs-ZnSnO3 composites may be attributed to the increased absorption of visible light as well as the improved separation of interface charge carrier. The possible mechanism of antibiotic photodegradation in the CDs-ZnSnO3 composite photocatalytic system was proposed by active species capture experiment and electron spin resonance (ESR) technique. Furthermore, the possible photodegradation pathway of TC was proposed on the basis of the intermediates detected by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) technology. This work provides a feasible strategy for effectively applying ZnSnO3-based photocatalyst to photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
ZnSnO3
Photocatalytic degradation
Tetracycline
Visible light

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of pristine ZSO and as-prepared CDs-ZSO composites.
(a) FT-IR spectra of ZSO, CDs and 3% CDs-ZSO. (b) Raman spectra of ZSO and 3% CDs-ZSO.
SEM images of (a, b) ZSO and (c) 3% CDs-ZSO. (d) The EDS spectrum of 3% CDs-ZSO.
(a, b) TEM images, (c) HRTEM analysis, (d) HAADF-STEM and (e–h) elemental mapping images of 3% CDs-ZSO photocatalyst.
high-resolution XPS spectra of (a) Zn 2p, (b) Sn 3d, (c) O 1s and (d) C 1s of pure ZSO and 3% CDs-ZSO composite.
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of as-prepared photocatalysts.

(a) Photodegradation of TC over as-prepared products under visible irradiation (λ > 420 nm). (b) Histogram showing comparative degradation rate (%) of TC. (c) First-order-kinetic plots and (d) apparent rate constants of TC degradation for the prepared samples. (e) Cycling runs for the photocatalytic TC degradation in the presence of 3% CDs-ZSO. (f) XRD patterns of 3% CDs-ZSO before and after 4 runs.

(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, (b) Room photoluminescence (PL) spectra, (b) Transient photocurrent responses and (c) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) changes of ZSO and 3% CDs-ZSO composite.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation rates of 3% CDs-ZSO in the presence of different scavengers. (b) ESR spectra for 3% CDs-ZSO in methanol dispersion for DMPO-
O2 −.
Photocatalytic mechanism of CDs-ZSO composite photocatalyst for the degradation TC under visible light irradiation.
Possible degradation pathway of TC in CDs-ZSO composite photocatalytic system.
Schematic illustration of the fabrication process over CDs-ZnSnO3 composite.
Comparison of photocatalytic efficiencies of other previously reported composite photocatalysts for degradation TC in recent years.
The structural information of the possible intermediates products.

head.ce:title: Investigation of visible-light-driven photocatalytic tetracycline degradation via carbon dots modified porous ZnSnO3 cubes: Mechanism and degradation pathway

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy and Power, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212003, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is highly desired to enhance the photocatalytic activity of ZnSnO3 owing to its potential application prospects in the photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics in water, such as the low cost, non-toxicity, simple preparation, although ZnSnO3 possesses wide band gap (~3.2 eV). Herein, a novel carbon dots (CDs) modified porous ZnSnO3 cubic photocatalyst was designed and synthesized using a simple precipitation-calcination method, which was to utilize the up-conversion photoluminescence performance and the ability of electronic transfer of CDs, further broadening the visible light absorption range of the photocatalyst and accelerating the separation of the electron-hole pairs. The experimental results indicate that with the optimal CDs loading (3 wt%), the CDs modified ZnSnO3 exhibits 21 times higher degradation kinetic towards tetracycline (TC) than pure ZnSnO3. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs-ZnSnO3 composites may be attributed to the increased absorption of visible light as well as the improved separation of interface charge carrier. The possible mechanism of antibiotic photodegradation in the CDs-ZnSnO3 composite photocatalytic system was proposed by active species capture experiment and electron spin resonance (ESR) technique. Furthermore, the possible photodegradation pathway of TC was proposed on the basis of the intermediates detected by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) technology. This work provides a feasible strategy for effectively applying ZnSnO3-based photocatalyst to photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics, as a typical common drug, have been extensively used in the treatment of diseases in humans and animals and in agricultural production activities [1–3]. However, due to the incomplete metabolism of antibiotics, most of them are discharged through feces and urine in the form of unmetabolized drugs or active metabolites, which are proverbially found in hospital wastewater, treated wastewater, surface water and groundwater [4,5]. There is ample evidence that residual antibiotics in the aquatic environment are biologically toxic and resistant, can cause numerous diseases and millions of deaths each year, and pose an extremely threat to animal and human health [6]. During the past few years, many researchers have explored various techniques to remove residual antibiotics in water, such as chemical oxidation, biodegradation, physical adsorption, photocatalysis, etc. [7–11]. Among them, photocatalysis has been considered as greener and more sustainable technology to use solar energy to degrade organic pollutants into non-toxic and harmless substances, which has raised considerable attention for the scholars [12–20]. Consequently, to design semiconductor photocatalysts with low cost, high stability and high-performance is of prerequisite in view of the practical applications.

A novel ABO3 photocatalytic material, zinc stannate (ZnSnO3), has been regarded as one of the most potential photocatalysts benefiting from its low-cost, thermal and chemical stability as well as non-toxicity [21–23]. Naturally, ZnSnO3-based photocatalysts have been applied in the removal of various of organic compounds in water [10,13]. In spite of extensive research on ZnSnO3, the photocatalytic performance of individual ZnSnO3 is still dissatisfactory, especially in the visible light (accounts for 43% of solar spectrum), owing to its low surface area, the rapid rate of photogenerated charge recombination and wide band-gap (3.23 eV). Consequently, much attention has been focused on how to enlarge its surface area, broaden its light absorption and separate its electron-hole pairs.

It is well known that the surface morphology of the semiconductor photocatalyst is closely related to its photocatalytic performance. Generally, the uniform morphology and medium size are considered to be the key factors to ameliorate its photocatalytic performance. Therefore, tremendous efforts have been devoted to design various morphologies of ZnSnO3, such as the ultra-thin ZnSnO3 nanowire and ZnSnO3 hollow nanospheres [24,25]. Among of them, the cubic ZnSnO3 with unique porous structure have become a hot spot recently owing to its larger surface area [26] and rich active sites [27]. Despite the photocatalytic activity can be improved to some extent through the adjustment of the morphologies of the ZnSnO3, a desirable photocatalytic performance of a single ZnSnO3 component in the visible light range still cannot be obtained because of the fast recombination of electron-hole pairs and wide band gap. Therefore, it is expected to further boost the photo-catalytic performance of ZnSnO3 by a variety of modification methods, which can not only improve the separation rate of photogenerated carriers, but also expand the visible light absorption region to make full use of solar energy.

Surface modification has been proven one of the most effective strategies. Especially, the introduction of cocatalysts onto the photocatalyst is more capable to separate the electron-hole pairs. Up to now, a serious of efficient noble-metal-based cocatalysts [28–30] and noble-metal-oxides cocatalysts [31–33] to modify semiconductors have been reported for enhanced prominently photocatalytic activity. Regrettably, the above noble-metal based cocatalysts are too rare and costly to be large-scale energy production. Accordingly, to search for the noble-metal free cocatalysts with high efficiency and low cost is more sensible.

Carbon nanodots (CDs), as a novel class of carbon nanomaterials with sizes of from 2 to 10 nm, have become a rising star due to their benign, non-toxic, inexpensive and eco-friendly characteristics [34–37]. Furthermore, CDs also exhibit some amazing properties as follows: (i) broad absorption spectrum and strong absorption coefficient [38]; (ii) unique electronic reservoir performance [39]; (iii) excellent chemical and thermal stability [40]. These remarkable characteristics provide new application prospects for CDs as a class of photoactive co-catalysts to improve photocatalytic activity and stability of semiconductors, such as Cu2O [41], Fe2O3 [42], Ag3PO4 [43], BiVO4 [34] and so on. Furthermore, it is encouraging that the introduction of CDs is considered as an excellent solution to enhance the visible-light photocatalytic performance of wide band-gap semiconductors due to its attractive up-conversion photoluminescence behavior [44]. For instance, Chen et al. modified BiOCOOH with CDs showed good photocatalytic activity and stability under visible light irradiation, and CDs-BiOCOOH (2 wt%) composites displayed the highest photocatalytic activity for diclofenac photodegradation, which is 4.64 times higher than that of the original BiOCOOH [44]. Considering such predominant properties of CDs and the limitations of ZnSnO3, we tried to design composites of decorating CDs with porous ZnSnO3 cubes for improving visible-light photocatalytic ability of ZnSnO3. To the best of our knowledge, no previous studies regarding CDs-ZnSnO3 composite, or their applications for environmental remediation have been reported.

In this work, CDs-ZnSnO3 composite photocatalyst was firstly synthesized by a simple precipitation-calcination method. The crystal structures, morphology, optical properties and stability were studied in detail through a series of characterization tests. The photocatalytic activity of CDs-ZnSnO3 composites was fully evaluated by the degradation of tetracycline (TC) under visible-light irradiation. Based on electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis and free radical capture experiments, the possible mechanism of photodegradation of pollutants over the CDs-ZnSnO3 composite photocatalytic system was investigated and proposed. Finally, the primary intermediates in the photodegradation of TC were detected by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) technique.

CDs were prepared by a reported electrochemical method of etching the graphite rods [45]. Using ultrapure water as the electrolyte, two graphite rods were used as the cathode and anode, respectively. After applying a voltage of 30 V to the two electrodes and continuously stirring for 120 h, a dark yellow solution gradually appeared in the reactor, which was due to the electrochemical etching of the anode graphite rod. Subsequently, the solution was filtered through a double-layered filter paper, and the precipitate was removed by high-speed centrifugation for 30 min to obtain the CDs solution with 500 mg/L. Finally, the CDs solution was freeze-dried to obtain the CDs powder.

The CDs-ZnSnO3 photocatalysts with different mass ratios were prepared by a precipitation-calcination method and shown in Scheme 1 . As 3% CDs-ZnSnO3 for example, at first, 0.474 g SnCl4·5H2O and 0.0081 g CDs were mixed in 60 mL of deionized water and stirred vigorously for 30 min to obtain a uniform suspension. Then, 3 M NaOH solution was slowly added dropwise to the above solution to adjust the pH value of the mixed solution to 12.0. Subsequently, 0.3885 g ZnSO4·7H2O was dissolved in the above-mentioned mixing system at a temperature of 60 °C under magnetic stirring for 5 min, followed by dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h. After that, the obtained precipitate was centrifuged, washed and dried. Finally, the obtained powder was calcined in a muffle furnace at 580 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 5 °C/min to prepare a 3% CDs-ZnSnO3 composite, abbreviated as 3% CDs-ZSO. Similar to the above preparation process, different mass ratios of CDs-ZnSnO3 composites (1 wt%, 2 wt%, 4 wt% and 5 wt%) were successfully synthesized and labeled as 1% CDs-ZSO, 2% CDs-ZSO, 4% CDs-ZSO and 5% CDs-ZSO, respectively. The pristine porous ZnSnO3 cube sample was fabricated according to the same procedure without CDs, and donated as ZSO. The photocatalytic experiment, active species trapping test and characterizations can be listed in the Supporting Information.

The crystal structure and phase purity of the prepared ZSO and a series of CDs-ZSO composite photocatalysts are provided by the X-ray diffractometer (XRD) patterns. As depicted in Fig. 1 , within the range of 10-80°, the peaks at 26.52°, 33.82°, 51.64° and 65.76° in ZSO can be indexed to (012), (110), (116) and (036) lattice planes, respectively, which is consistent with the ZSO (JCPDS 28-1486) [46]. Noticeably, after modifying ZSO with CDs, the XRD patterns showed little change compared with pure ZSO, which fully indicated that the introduction of CDs had no effect on the crystal structure of the ZSO. Moreover, no diffraction peaks of CDs were observed due to the relatively small content, low crystallinity and good dispersion in composite [47].

The composition and chemical bonds of as-prepared materials were investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. Fig. 2 a shows that the absorption peaks at 405 and 649 cm−1 belong to the ZnO and SnO stretching vibration modes in the ZSO nanostructure [48], while the absorption peaks around at 3435 and 1439 cm−1 correspond to OH stretching vibration and OH bending of H2O molecules absorbed to the surface [48]. For 3% CDs-ZSO composite, the absorption peaks are very similar to those of pure ZSO samples. Notability, an absorption peak appeared at 1634 cm−1, which is ascribed to the characteristic peak of CO, revealing the existence of CDs in the composite [40]. In addition, the Raman spectra of ZSO and 3% CDs-ZSO samples were carried out for comparison. As shown in Fig. 2b, the D and G bands of the two prominent Raman peaks at 1326 and 1602 cm−1 can be clearly observed in the Raman spectrum of the 3% CDs-ZSO composite, further indicating the existence of CDs [49].

The morphologies of as-prepared pristine ZSO and 3% CDs-ZSO composite were investigated by rely on scanning transmission electron microscopy (SEM) technique. From Fig. 3 a and b, the pristine ZSO cubes have smooth surfaces with average diameter of around 200 nm. Besides, Fig. 3c exhibits the SEM image of 3% CDs-ZSO composite, which presents similar cubic morphology with pristine ZSO, indicating that the introduction of CDs has no effect on the morphology of ZSO. Noticeably, the morphology of CDs cannot be clearly observed because the CDs nanoparticles are really too small (the following TEM and HRTEM tests confirmed the existence of CDs). In Fig. 3d, through the measurement of EDS, it was analyzed that the CDs-ZSO sample was composed of Zn, Sn, O and C elements.

The microstructure of as-synthesized photocatalyst were characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analyses. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the 3% CDs-ZSO composite exhibits a uniform micron-sized cubic structure, which the edge lengths are in the range of 70–100 nm. Furthermore, the obvious contrast between black and pale images in the cube fully demonstrates that the 3% CDs-ZSO sample is classified as porous structure, and the CDs are evenly dispersed on the surface of the porous ZSO cubes (red circles in Fig. 4b). From the HRTEM image, as shown in Fig. 4c, the existence of a tight interface between CDs and ZSO is clearly found in the composite. The lattice spacing of CDs is determined to be 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of carbon [50], and the lattice spacing around 0.26 nm can also be found, which is in good agreement with the crystallographic (110) spacing of ZSO [46]. Meanwhile, the HAADF-STEM image and the corresponding element mapping images of 3% CDs-ZSO were performed in Fig. 4d–h, displaying Zn, Sn, O, and C elements are well dispersed within the CDs-ZSO composite. Because the ultrathin carbon film acts as a test substrate, it has a slight effect on the detection of C elements (Fig. 4h). The above results further reveal that the CDs have been perfectly modified on the porous ZSO cubes to construct CDs-ZSO composite photocatalyst.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the effect of introducing CDs on the surface chemical state of ZSO. The XPS survey scan reveals that Zn, Sn, O and C are the predominant elements in the CDs-ZSO composite (Fig. S1). As demonstrated in Fig. 5 a, for pure ZSO sample, two major peaks of Zn 2p located at 1021.2 eV and 1044.3 eV can be corresponded to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively [51]. In Fig. 5b, the XPS peaks at 486.2 and 494.7 eV are attributed to the Sn 3d5/2 and Sn 3d3/2 for Sn4+ [52]. As illustrated in Fig. 5c, the XPS spectrum of high-resolution O 1s can be decomposed into two peaks, located at 529.8 eV and 531.1 eV, corresponding to the metal oxide bond and the surface OH groups [53]. The C 1 s spectrum of CDs-ZSO composite is shown in Fig. 5d, where the signal peak at 284.5 eV can be attributed to the CC/CC of the graphite structure of CDs, while the binding energies of around 285.5 and 288.5 eV are assigned to the CO and CO bond, respectively [49]. Notably, the binding energies of Zn 2p, Sn 3d and O 1s in 3% CDs-ZSO composite were positively shifted to higher values compared with pure ZSO, indicating that there may be chemical interactions and electron migration between CDs and ZSO.

To further explore the capability of ZSO to be modified by CDs, the optical properties of as-fabricated pure ZSO and CDs-ZSO materials were examined with the UV–vis DRS analysis. Fig. 6 shows that the pure ZSO photocatalyst exhibited a limited absorption edge at 390 nm, implying that the photocatalyst was exclusively activated by ultraviolet light [46]. Obviously, compared with pure ZSO, the absorption capacity of CDs-ZSO composite photocatalysts in the visible light region is significantly improved due to the presence of CDs [54]. Noticeably, it can also be clearly observed that the absorption efficiency of visible light gradually strengthened with the increase loading amount of CDs, which is helpful for the efficient use of light by photocatalyst. The photos of pure ZSO and CDs-ZSO samples are shown in Fig. S2. Obviously, with the load of CDs increases, the samples change from white to dark gray.

The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore size distributions of pure ZSO and 3% CDs-ZSO composites have been investigated using N2 adsorption-desorption measurements at 77 K and illustrated in Fig. 8 a. The results showed that combined with the brunauer-deming-demingteller (BDDT) classification, all the as-synthesized materials have type IV isotherms, revealing the presence of mesopores in the samples [62]. The BET surface area of the obtained 3% CDs-ZSO (36.083 m2/g) was significantly larger than that of pure ZSO (34.555 m2/g), indicating that the introduction of CDs was favorable to provide more active sites for the surface redox reaction of the reactants. Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra can be used to estimate the recombination behavior of photogenerated electrons and holes in semiconductor photocatalysts. The PL intensity depends on the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers in semiconductor materials. Therefore, the reduced fluorescence intensity represents the high charge carrier separation efficiency of semiconductor materials [63,64]. The PL spectra of ZSO and 3% CDs-ZSO composites were measured, and the results are depicted in Fig. 8b. The PL quenching phenomenon of the modified 3% CDs-ZSO composites occurred at 350–600 nm. Obviously, photoelectrons may transition from the ZSO conduction band to CDs, limiting the electron-hole pair recombination. The above results provide reliable support for the conclusion that the degradation efficiency of the CDs-ZSO composite photocatalyst is higher than that of pure ZSO. To further inspect the low recombination rate of 3% CDs-ZSO composites with photogenerated electrons and holes, photocurrent measurements of pure ZSO and 3% CDs-ZSO composites were performed. As shown in Fig. 8c, the photocurrent density of 3% CDs-ZSO composites increased significantly compared to the pure component materials. It is further proved that the introduction of CDs significantly enhances the charge carrier separation of ZSO, which is an important factor for improving the photoelectric response. Moreover, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis of ZSO and 3% CDs-ZSO materials is displayed in Fig. 8d. The smaller arc radius represents the lower charge transfer resistance [46,65]. Obviously, 3% CDs-ZSO composite exhibit the smaller semicircle radius compared with pure ZSO samples. These observations show that the composites possess an excellent interfacial charge transfer performance.

Radical scavenge experiment was performed to analyzed the roles of active species during the photocatalytic degradation of TC. Ethylene diamine tetra-acetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), isopropyl (IPA) and nitrogen (N2) were applied as the trapping agents for h+,
OH and
O2 −, respectively [38]. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the addition of IPA to the decomposition process of TC is still strong (78.16% photodegradation), while N2 and EDTA displayed significantly weaken, in which the degradation efficiency are reduced to 11.23% and 14.68%, respectively. The above results indicate that
O2 − and h+ play the key roles in the photocatalytic degradation of TC. In order to further verify the existence of active species in the photocatalytic process, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrum was performed on 3% CDs-ZSO composite with dimethyl pyridine N-oxide (DMPO) as the scavengers. The test was conducted to under the condition of dark or visible light irradiation. As exhibited in Fig. 9b, the prepared photocatalyst exist no obvious peak of
O2 − in the dark, but a clear and strong characteristic signal of DMPO-
O2 − can be observed under visible light irradiation, indicating that
O2 − was generated during photodegradation over CDs-ZSO composite. As a consequence,
O2 − and h+ can be generated during the photoreaction process and play an important role in the degradation of TC, which is agreement with the corresponding capture experiments.

Based on the results of the above free radical capture and ESR examinations, a possible mechanism for photocatalytic degradation TC of CDs-ZSO composites was proposed and displayed in Fig. 10 . Firstly, the optical band gaps of ZSO sample can be estimated from the Tauc plot, which are derived from the the UV–vis DRS technology. The corresponding calculation is based on the Kubelka-Munk function: (αhv) = A(hv Eg ) n/2
[66,67], where α, ʋ and Eg can be ascribed to absorption coefficient, frequency of the incident light and band gap energy of the sample, A and h are the adsorption constants, (n in the index is equal to 1 for direct band gap, 4 for indirect band gap of this work) [46]. Thus, the band gap energy (Eg ) of ZSO was determined to be 3.23 eV (Fig. S5a). Additionally, the Mott-Schottky diagram is applied to determine the electronic band structure of as-fabricated ZSO sample. As demonstrated in Fig. S5b, the flat band potential of ZSO is −0.05 V, and ZSO is an n-type semiconductor with a positive slope in the linear graph. Generally, the potential of the conduction band (CB) has a more negative value of approximate 0.1 or 0.2 V compared to its flat band potential in n-type semiconductors [68]. Therefore, the CB potential of the ZSO is estimated to be −0.15 eV. After that, the EVB of ZSO was calculated as 3.08 eV according to the formula of EVB = ECB + Eg
[69,70]. When the CDs-ZSO composite photocatalysts were illuminated with visible light, the CDs can convert long-wavelength (λ > 500 nm) light to shorter-wavelength (λ < 500 nm) light owing to its up-converted PL properties [44], which is subsequently absorbed by the ZSO. These photoinduced light at the shorter-wavelengths can in turn excite ZSO to form electron-hole pairs [71]. The photoelectrons excited by ZSO can be rapidly transferred from its CB to CDs, and these electrons can then reduce O2 to
O2 − (−0.046 eV for O2/
O2 −) in aqueous solution, which is a strong oxidizing agent to quickly degrade TC. At the same time, CDs as electron reservoir can inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. In addition, h+ remaining in VB of ZSO can take part in the organic pollutants degradation directly. Hence, the CDs-ZSO composite photocatalysts exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity compared to the pure ZSO.

To deepen the understanding of the TC photocatalytic degradation process, the degradation intermediates of TC were performed using LC-MS analysis, and a possible degradation pathway of TC in CDs-ZSO composite photocatalytic system was proposed. The LC-MS spectra and proposed molecular structure of the degradation products of 3% CDs-ZSO photocatalyst (after 60 min degradation) are depicted in Fig. S6 and Table 2 . The corresponding peak of the TC molecule is significantly reduced, which is due to the photodegradation of TC during the photocatalysis, and some new peaks appear in the degradation intermediates (such as m/z = 464, 430, 416, 392, 358, 306, 278, 230, 149, 106). Combining the intermediate products detected on mass spectrometry and related reports [72], two possible TC degradation pathways were proposed, as shown in Fig. 11 . Pathway I is mainly due to the positive holes being able to receive electrons from TC and the cleavage of different functional groups to form a series of intermediates [72]. Generally, due to the weak bond energy of N-C, the N-demethylation of TC can easily occur, which results in the intermediate products TC1 (m/z = 430) and TC2 (m/z = 416) [5]. Subsequently, the amino and acylamino group were further shed to obtain the intermediate TC3 (m/z = 358). Pathway II is because the TC pollutant in the water is firstly attacked by
O2 − radicals, causing the two CC bonds to be destroyed, and then invaded by
OH radicals, resulting in a hydroxylation reaction, which forms TC4 (m/z = 464) [73]. Besides, the h+ produced in the system attacks the amino and amido groups, forming an intermediate product TC5 (m/z = 392). With the ongoing of degradation process, because of the strong oxidation of
O2 −, the products TC3 and TC5 were oxidatively decomposed and a ring-opening reaction occurred to obtain an intermediate of TC6 (m/z = 306) [74]. The product TC7 (m/z = 278) was detected by losing two methyl groups in the carbon atom ring under further aggression by the active species. Subsequently, as some functional groups (acids and alcohols) on the carbocycle continued to fall off, the intermediate product TC7 was gradually degraded to TC8 (m/z = 230). After that, TC8 was continuously decomposed to aromatic compounds TC9 (m/z = 149) and TC10 (m/z = 106). With increasing reaction time, oxidative decomposition and ring-opening reactions further occur, and the above intermediate products are finally decomposed into H2O, CO2 and NH4 +, etc.

In summary, a new type of CDs-ZSO composite photocatalyst has been successfully developed using a simple precipitation-calcination method. Under visible light irradiation, the 3% CDs-ZSO composites exhibited superior photocatalytic performance and stability for the degradation of tetracycline, which may be due to the electron transfer ability and up-conversion photoluminescence performance of the CDs were conducive to improving this photocatalysis active. In addition, the unique porous cubic structure of ZnSnO3 makes better use of the incident light by light reflections, providing large specific surface area and rich active sites for photocatalytic reactions. Based on capture experiments and ESR measurements, the results show that
O2 − and h+ play a leading role in the photocatalytic reaction. Combining the active species introduced in the above test with the reaction intermediates measured by LC-MS, the photocatalytic reaction mechanism of CDs-ZSO was revealed in detail and the degradation pathway of tetracycline in aqueous solution was present. This research provides numerous insights into the design and preparation of new photocatalysts, which may be of great significance for future environmental remediation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: National Natural Science Foundations of China (No. 21906072), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (C) (BK20190982), Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Jiangsu University of Science and Technology (China) (1062931806 and 1142931803).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2020.117518.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Z. Cetecioglu, B. lnce, M. Gros, S. Rodriguez-Mozaz, D. Barcelo, D. Orhon, O. lnce, Water Res. 47 (2013) 2959–2969.
C.V. Gómez-Pacheco, M. Sánchez-Polo, J. Rivera-Utrilla, J. López-Pe Alver, Chem. Eng. J. 178 (2011) 115–121.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Guo, W. Shi, M. Li, Y. Shi, H. Wen, Sep. Purif. Technol., 210 (2019) 608-615.
